seoTitle: Technical Support - Aipu Power Module Solutions | BeiLuo Distributor
seoDescription: Aipu power module technical support center, providing product selection guides, application notes, design references, and troubleshooting resources.
seoKeywords: Aipu, power module, technical support, DC-DC converter, AC-DC power supply, isolated transceiver, application note

faqs.question: How to select the right power module for my application?
faqs.answer: Selecting the right power module involves several key steps: 1) Determine input voltage - stable voltage (use fixed input) or varying voltage (use wide input); 2) Calculate output power - sum all load power consumption and add 20-30% margin; 3) Choose isolation level - 1500VDC for general industrial, 2500VDC for power/medical; 4) Select package - SIP for compact space, DIP for harsh environments; 5) Consider special requirements - medical grade, extended temperature, low ripple. Use our selection guides for detailed guidance on DC-DC, AC-DC, and isolated transceiver modules.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use our selection guides for DC-DC, AC-DC, and isolated transceivers. Contact FAE team for complex applications.

faqs.question: What is the difference between fixed input and wide input DC-DC modules?
faqs.answer: Fixed input DC-DC modules (A series) have specific input voltage (5V, 12V, 24V) with +/-10% tolerance, suitable for stable power supplies. They offer higher efficiency (82-86%) and lower cost. Wide input DC-DC modules (B series) have 4:1 input range (such as 9-36V), suitable for battery power and voltage-fluctuating applications. They provide better flexibility but slightly lower efficiency (80-84%). Choose fixed input for stable voltages, wide input for batteries and fluctuating voltages.
faqs.decisionGuide: Fixed input for stable voltages (higher efficiency, lower cost). Wide input for batteries and fluctuating voltages (more flexibility).

faqs.question: Do I need isolation for my power supply design?
faqs.answer: Isolation is required when: 1) Ground loops exist - multiple ground points causing noise coupling; 2) Safety protection needed - preventing high-voltage faults from reaching low-voltage side; 3) Noise immunity required - blocking common-mode interference; 4) Level shifting - converting between different voltage domains; 5) Medical/safety standards - IEC 60601-1 or similar requiring patient isolation. Isolation is essential in industrial control, medical equipment, communication systems, and power monitoring. For simple, non-critical applications with single ground reference, non-isolated modules may be sufficient.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use isolation for industrial, medical, communication, and multi-ground systems. Non-isolated may work for simple single-ground applications.

faqs.question: How to contact BeiLuo technical support?
faqs.answer: BeiLuo provides comprehensive technical support for Aipu power modules: 1) Phone support: Call our technical hotline during business hours for immediate assistance; 2) Email support: Send detailed questions to our FAE team for written responses; 3) Online resources: Access application notes, selection guides, and FAQs on our website; 4) Sample request: Request evaluation samples for testing in your application; 5) Custom solutions: Contact us for specialized requirements or custom designs. Our FAE team has extensive experience in power module applications and can help with selection, design review, troubleshooting, and optimization.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact us by phone for urgent issues, email for detailed technical questions, or request samples for evaluation.

faqs.question: What is the typical delivery time for Aipu power modules?
faqs.answer: Aipu power modules are available from BeiLuo with the following typical delivery times: 1) Standard products: 1-2 weeks for stocked items, 4-6 weeks for non-stocked standard products; 2) High-volume orders: 6-8 weeks depending on quantity and production schedule; 3) Custom products: 8-12 weeks for modified specifications or custom designs; 4) Sample orders: 3-5 days for small quantity samples from stock. We maintain inventory of popular models for quick delivery. For urgent requirements, please contact our sales team to check current stock availability and expedite options.
faqs.decisionGuide: Standard products 1-6 weeks, samples 3-5 days. Contact sales for stock availability and expedited delivery.

faqs.question: What is the warranty period for Aipu power modules?
faqs.answer: Aipu power modules come with a standard 3-year warranty from BeiLuo. The warranty covers defects in materials and workmanship under normal use conditions. During the warranty period, defective modules will be repaired or replaced free of charge. The warranty does not cover damage caused by misuse, overstress, improper installation, or unauthorized modifications. For critical applications, extended warranty options are available. Please contact our sales team for warranty details and terms. Proper application and installation according to our guidelines will ensure optimal performance and reliability throughout the product lifetime.
faqs.decisionGuide: Standard 3-year warranty covers material and workmanship defects. Extended warranty available for critical applications.

faqs.question: Can I get samples for evaluation before ordering?
faqs.answer: Yes, BeiLuo provides evaluation samples for Aipu power modules. Sample requests can be submitted through our website or by contacting our sales team. Samples are typically shipped within 3-5 business days from stock. For standard products, up to 5 samples can be provided free of charge for qualified customers. For high-volume or custom products, sample fees may apply which can be credited against future orders. Our FAE team can provide technical support during your evaluation to ensure the selected module meets your application requirements. Contact us to discuss your specific needs and sample availability.
faqs.decisionGuide: Free samples available for qualified customers. Contact sales or submit request through website. FAE support provided during evaluation.

faqs.question: What technical documentation is available for Aipu power modules?
faqs.answer: BeiLuo provides comprehensive technical documentation for Aipu power modules: 1) Datasheets: Detailed electrical specifications, mechanical dimensions, and performance curves; 2) Application notes: Design guidelines, selection guides, and application-specific recommendations; 3) Reference designs: Schematics, PCB layouts, and BOM for typical applications; 4) Safety certificates: UL, EN, CCC, CE, CB certifications; 5) Reliability reports: MTBF, life test data, and failure analysis; 6) 3D models: STEP files for mechanical integration. All documentation is available for download from our website or can be requested from our technical support team. Custom documentation can be provided for specific requirements.
faqs.decisionGuide: Complete documentation including datasheets, app notes, reference designs, and certifications available on website or upon request.

articles.title: Power Module Isolation Design Guide
articles.description: Detailed introduction to power module isolation design principles, isolation voltage selection, and isolation system application methods
articles.shortDescription: Power module isolation design principles and applications
content: This technical reference document provides detailed information about aipu product specifications, characteristics, and performance parameters. Use this information to support your design and analysis activities.

Electrical characteristics are specified over the operating temperature range unless otherwise noted. Parameters are guaranteed by design, testing, or statistical analysis. Typical values represent the most likely parametric norm at 25°C.

Thermal characteristics require careful attention during system design. The junction-to-ambient thermal resistance depends on the mounting configuration, PCB copper area, and airflow conditions. Use thermal simulation tools to predict operating temperatures under actual conditions.

Reliability data is based on accelerated life testing and field failure analysis. Mean time between failures (MTBF) calculations follow industry-standard methodologies. Contact BeiLuo for detailed reliability reports and qualification data.

sections.heading: Basic Principles of Isolation
sections.content: Power module isolation is achieved through transformers, completely separating input and output circuits electrically. Isolation can block ground loops, prevent noise coupling, protect sensitive circuits, and improve system safety. Isolated power modules contain isolation transformers, rectifier circuits, feedback circuits, etc.

sections.heading: Isolation Voltage Level Selection
sections.content: Select appropriate isolation voltage levels according to application requirements: 1000VDC for general industrial control; 1500VDC for standard industrial applications; 2500VDC for power systems and medical equipment; 3000VDC for high-voltage equipment and medical devices with direct patient contact.

sections.heading: Grounding Design for Isolation Systems
sections.content: Grounding design for isolation systems is very important: Input and output sides are grounded separately to avoid ground loops; Sensitive circuits use isolated power supply to prevent noise interference; Communication interfaces use isolated transceivers to achieve signal isolation.

sections.heading: Applications of Isolated Power Modules
sections.content: Typical applications of isolated power modules: Signal isolation in industrial control systems, patient isolation in medical equipment, port isolation in communication equipment, measurement isolation in power systems, etc. Selection should consider isolation voltage, power, efficiency, size and other factors.

sections.heading: Testing and Verification of Isolation Systems
sections.content: Testing of isolation systems includes: Insulation resistance testing (verifying isolation performance), withstand voltage testing (verifying isolation strength), functional testing (verifying normal operation). Testing should follow safety procedures and use dedicated test equipment.

faqs.question: Why is isolated power supply needed?
faqs.answer: The main functions of isolated power supply are: 1) Blocking ground loops: When the system has multiple ground points, isolation can cut off ground loops to prevent noise coupling through ground wires; 2) Protecting sensitive circuits: Isolating sensitive analog circuits from noisy digital circuits to improve signal quality; 3) Safety protection: Preventing high-voltage side faults from affecting the low-voltage side, protecting personnel and equipment safety; 4) Level conversion: Achieving power conversion and isolation between different voltage level systems; 5) Anti-interference: Isolation can block common-mode interference to improve system anti-interference capability. Isolation is essential in industrial control, medical equipment, communication systems and other applications.
faqs.decisionGuide: Industrial control, medical equipment, communication systems and other applications must use isolated power supply.

faqs.question: What is the difference between 1500VDC and 2500VDC isolation voltage?
faqs.answer: The main differences between 1500VDC and 2500VDC isolation voltage are isolation strength and applicable occasions: 1500VDC is the standard industrial isolation level, suitable for general industrial control, instrumentation, communication equipment, etc., capable of withstanding 1500VDC test voltage for 1 minute without breakdown; 2500VDC is the high isolation level, suitable for power systems, medical equipment, high-voltage equipment, etc., capable of withstanding 2500VDC test voltage for 1 minute without breakdown. Selection basis: General industrial applications can select 1500VDC, power and medical applications need 2500VDC or higher. Higher isolation voltage means thicker insulation layers and higher costs.
faqs.decisionGuide: Select 1500VDC for general industrial applications, 2500VDC or higher for power and medical applications.

faqs.question: What is the efficiency of isolated power modules?
faqs.answer: The efficiency of isolated power modules is usually between 80%-90%, depending on topology structure, power level, input-output voltage ratio and other factors. Fixed input isolated module efficiency is about 82-86%, wide input isolated module efficiency is about 80-84%, AC-DC isolated module efficiency is about 85-90%. Efficiency losses mainly come from: transformer losses (copper loss and iron loss), rectifier diode voltage drop, switching losses, control circuit power consumption. Methods to improve efficiency: Select high-efficiency topology (such as flyback, forward), use synchronous rectification, optimize transformer design, reduce switching frequency (but will increase size). Efficiency is particularly important for battery-powered applications.
faqs.decisionGuide: Isolated power efficiency is usually 80-90%, selecting high-efficiency models can reduce power consumption and heat generation.

faqs.question: How to test the isolation performance of isolated power supply?
faqs.answer: Methods for testing isolation performance of isolated power supply: 1) Insulation resistance test: Use a megohmmeter (500V or 1000V range) to measure insulation resistance between input and output, should be greater than 100M ohms; 2) Withstand voltage test (Hi-Pot): Use a withstand voltage tester to apply specified test voltage (such as 1500VDC or 2500VDC) for 1 minute, leakage current should be less than 1mA, no breakdown; 3) Functional test: Isolated power supply works normally, output voltage is stable; 4) Isolation capacitance test: Measure parasitic capacitance between input and output, generally 10-100pF. Test precautions: Ensure power module is not powered before testing, increase test voltage gradually from 0, discharge after testing.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use megohmmeter and withstand voltage tester for insulation resistance and withstand voltage testing to verify isolation performance.

faqs.question: What is the impact of isolation capacitance in isolated power modules?
faqs.answer: The isolation capacitance (parasitic capacitance between input and output) of isolated power modules will produce the following effects: 1) Common-mode noise coupling: High-frequency common-mode noise can couple to the output side through isolation capacitance, affecting sensitive circuits; 2) Reduced isolation: The larger the isolation capacitance, the lower the high-frequency isolation; 3) EMI issues: Isolation capacitance and common-mode inductance form resonance, which may produce EMI issues. Isolation capacitance typical value is 10-100pF, depending on transformer structure and shielding design. Methods to reduce isolation capacitance: Use shielded transformers, increase isolation distance, optimize winding structure. For high-frequency communication applications, low isolation capacitance power modules need to be selected.
faqs.decisionGuide: For sensitive applications, select low isolation capacitance modules, or use shielding and filtering to reduce common-mode noise.

faqs.question: How to use isolated transceiver modules with isolated power supply?
faqs.answer: Isolated transceiver modules (such as CAN, RS-485 isolated transceivers) usually require isolated power supply. Methods of use: 1) Independent isolated power supply: Use independent DC-DC isolated power modules to supply power to transceivers, such as A0505S-1W providing 5V isolated power; 2) Integrated isolated power supply: Some transceiver modules integrate isolated power supply internally, no external power supply required; 3) Power supply design: Transceiver side power supply capacity should meet transceiver power consumption requirements, usually 1W is sufficient; 4) Grounding design: Transceiver side is grounded separately, isolated from master control side; 5) Filtering design: Add decoupling capacitors (0.1uF+10uF) at transceiver power pins. Aipu's CTM series CAN isolated transceivers and RSM series RS-485 isolated transceivers integrate isolated power supply, very convenient to use.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use integrated isolated power supply transceiver modules to simplify design, or use with independent isolated power modules.

author.name: BeiLuo FAE Team
author.title: Senior Application Engineer
author.bio: Has more than 10 years of power module application experience, focusing on isolation design and system applications.

articles.summary: This application note introduces the isolation design principles and application methods of power modules, helping engineers correctly apply isolated power modules.

faeInsights.content: Our FAE team has found in actual work that many customers do not have a deep enough understanding of isolated power supply, and are prone to selection errors or application problems. This Power Module Isolation Design Guide systematically introduces the principles, selection methods and application techniques of isolation, and the content is very practical. We particularly emphasize the selection of isolation voltage levels, grounding design of isolation systems, testing and verification of isolation performance and other key content, which are areas prone to errors in actual projects. For applications using isolated transceivers, we also introduce how to use them with isolated power supply. This article can help engineers quickly master the key points of isolated power supply design and avoid common mistakes. For complex isolation applications, it is recommended to contact our FAE team for professional support.
faeInsights.insightLogic: Based on a large number of customer consultations and project experience, this article covers the key knowledge points of isolated power supply design and has strong practicality.
keyTakeaways: Understand isolation principles and voltage level selection
Master grounding design for isolation systems
Learn isolation testing and verification methods
Know how to use isolated transceivers with power supply
commonPitfalls: Selecting wrong isolation voltage for application
Neglecting isolation capacitance in high-frequency applications
bestPractices: Always verify isolation requirements before selection
Separate grounding for input and output sides
Test isolation performance with proper equipment
Consider isolation capacitance for sensitive circuits
troubleshootingTips: Use megohmmeter to check insulation resistance
Perform Hi-Pot test for isolation strength verification
Check for ground loops with multimeter
Measure isolation capacitance for high-frequency issues

customerCases.challenge: Equipment leakage current exceeded standards, unable to pass medical certification
customerCases.solution: Redesigned isolated power supply system, improved isolation level, optimized grounding
customerCases.feedback: Leakage current reduced to below standard, successfully passed medical certification

articles.title: DC-DC Power Module Selection Guide
articles.description: Comprehensive guide for selecting DC-DC power modules including fixed input and wide input types
articles.shortDescription: How to select the right DC-DC power module for your application
content: This technical reference document provides detailed information about aipu product specifications, characteristics, and performance parameters. Use this information to support your design and analysis activities.

Electrical characteristics are specified over the operating temperature range unless otherwise noted. Parameters are guaranteed by design, testing, or statistical analysis. Typical values represent the most likely parametric norm at 25°C.

Thermal characteristics require careful attention during system design. The junction-to-ambient thermal resistance depends on the mounting configuration, PCB copper area, and airflow conditions. Use thermal simulation tools to predict operating temperatures under actual conditions.

Reliability data is based on accelerated life testing and field failure analysis. Mean time between failures (MTBF) calculations follow industry-standard methodologies. Contact BeiLuo for detailed reliability reports and qualification data.

sections.heading: Understanding DC-DC Module Types
sections.content: Aipu offers two main types of DC-DC modules: Fixed input modules (A series) with specific input voltages (5V, 12V, 24V) and wide input modules (B series) with 4:1 input range. Fixed input modules offer higher efficiency and lower cost for stable voltage applications. Wide input modules provide flexibility for battery-powered and voltage-fluctuating applications.

sections.heading: Power Level Selection
sections.content: Select power level based on load requirements: Calculate total power consumption of all devices, add 20-30% margin for reliable operation, consider startup current and transient loads. Aipu offers 0.25W to 60W options. Higher power modules have better thermal performance and longer lifetime when not operated at maximum rating continuously.

sections.heading: Package Selection
sections.content: SIP (Single In-line Package) offers compact size for space-constrained applications. DIP (Dual In-line Package) provides better mechanical stability and heat dissipation for harsh environments. SMD (Surface Mount Device) enables automated assembly for high-volume production.

faqs.question: When should I choose fixed input vs wide input DC-DC modules?
faqs.answer: Choose fixed input (A series) when: Input voltage is stable (such as regulated 5V, 12V, or 24V); Maximum efficiency is required; Cost is a primary concern; Space is limited. Choose wide input (B series) when: Powering from batteries with voltage variation; Input voltage fluctuates significantly; System needs to work with multiple input voltages; Flexibility is more important than maximum efficiency. Fixed input modules typically have 2-3% higher efficiency and 10-15% lower cost than equivalent wide input modules.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use fixed input for stable voltages and maximum efficiency. Use wide input for batteries and fluctuating voltages.

faqs.question: How do I calculate the required power level?
faqs.answer: Calculate required power level: 1) Sum power consumption of all devices powered by the module (P = V × I for each device); 2) Add 20-30% margin: P_module = P_total × 1.2 to 1.3; 3) Consider startup current: Some devices draw 2-3× normal current at startup; 4) Account for temperature: At high ambient temperatures (>50°C), derate by 20-30%; 5) Select next standard power level above calculated value. Example: For devices totaling 1.5W, with 30% margin = 1.95W, select 2W or 3W module. It's better to have excess capacity than to operate at maximum rating continuously.
faqs.decisionGuide: Calculate total load power, add 20-30% margin, select next available standard power level.

faqs.question: What isolation voltage do I need for my application?
faqs.answer: Select isolation voltage based on application requirements: 1) 1000VDC: Suitable for general industrial control with low isolation requirements; 2) 1500VDC: Standard industrial grade, suitable for most industrial control, instrumentation, and communication equipment; 3) 2500VDC: High isolation grade for power systems, medical equipment, and high-voltage equipment; 4) 3000VDC: Medical grade for equipment with direct patient contact. Higher isolation voltage provides better safety margin and noise immunity but at higher cost. For most industrial applications, 1500VDC is sufficient. For medical or high-voltage applications, 2500VDC or 3000VDC is required.
faqs.decisionGuide: 1500VDC for general industrial, 2500VDC for power/medical, 3000VDC for patient contact medical equipment.

faqs.question: SIP vs DIP package - which should I choose?
faqs.answer: Choose SIP (Single In-line Package) when: Space is limited and compact design is required; Cost is a primary concern; Application environment is clean and stable; PCB density is high. Choose DIP (Dual In-line Package) when: Mechanical stability is important; Application has vibration or shock; Better heat dissipation is needed; Long-term reliability is critical. SIP packages are smaller (typically 11.5mm wide) and lower cost. DIP packages are larger (typically 19-22mm wide) but provide better mechanical strength and thermal performance. For most industrial applications, DIP is recommended for better reliability.
faqs.decisionGuide: SIP for compact, cost-sensitive applications. DIP for harsh environments requiring better mechanical stability and heat dissipation.

faqs.question: How does temperature affect power module performance?
faqs.answer: Temperature affects power module performance in several ways: 1) Efficiency decreases at high temperatures due to increased semiconductor losses; 2) Maximum output power must be derated above 50°C ambient to prevent overheating; 3) Lifetime decreases with higher operating temperatures - every 10°C increase halves the lifetime; 4) Input voltage range may narrow at temperature extremes. Typical derating: 100% load at 25°C, 80% load at 50°C, 60% load at 70°C. Always ensure adequate ventilation and consider heat sinking for high-power or high-temperature applications. The module's internal overtemperature protection will shut down the output if temperature exceeds safe limits.
faqs.decisionGuide: Derate power at high temperatures. Ensure adequate cooling for reliable operation. Consider DIP packages for better heat dissipation.

author.name: Zhang Wei
author.title: Power Application Engineer
author.bio: 8 years experience in DC-DC power module applications and design support.

articles.summary: Comprehensive guide for selecting DC-DC power modules based on application requirements.

faeInsights.content: In my 8 years supporting DC-DC power module applications, I've seen many selection errors that could have been avoided with proper analysis. The most common mistake is selecting power level too close to actual load - always add margin for reliable operation. Another frequent issue is choosing fixed input for battery applications where wide input is needed. I always recommend customers calculate worst-case power consumption including startup currents and temperature effects. For industrial applications, consider DIP packages for better reliability. This selection guide covers the key decision points I discuss with customers daily.
faeInsights.insightLogic: Proper power module selection requires understanding load characteristics, environmental conditions, and application requirements.
keyTakeaways: Match input type to power source characteristics
commonPitfalls: Using fixed input for battery applications
troubleshootingTips: Measure actual load current vs calculated
Check input voltage stability under load

customerCases.challenge: Frequent module failures due to insufficient power margin and high temperature
customerCases.solution: Recalculated power requirements with proper margin, upgraded to higher power module with better thermal rating
customerCases.feedback: System reliability improved significantly, no failures in 2 years of operation

articles.title: EMI Design Guide for Power Modules
articles.description: Best practices for EMI reduction and EMC compliance in power module applications
articles.shortDescription: EMI reduction techniques and EMC compliance for power modules
content: This technical reference document provides detailed information about aipu product specifications, characteristics, and performance parameters. Use this information to support your design and analysis activities.

Electrical characteristics are specified over the operating temperature range unless otherwise noted. Parameters are guaranteed by design, testing, or statistical analysis. Typical values represent the most likely parametric norm at 25°C.

Thermal characteristics require careful attention during system design. The junction-to-ambient thermal resistance depends on the mounting configuration, PCB copper area, and airflow conditions. Use thermal simulation tools to predict operating temperatures under actual conditions.

Reliability data is based on accelerated life testing and field failure analysis. Mean time between failures (MTBF) calculations follow industry-standard methodologies. Contact BeiLuo for detailed reliability reports and qualification data.

sections.heading: Understanding EMI in Power Modules
sections.content: Switching power modules generate EMI through high-frequency switching, diode reverse recovery, and magnetic coupling. Conducted EMI (150kHz-30MHz) travels through power lines. Radiated EMI (30MHz-1GHz) emits from circuits and cables. Understanding EMI sources is essential for effective mitigation.

sections.heading: Input Filtering Design
sections.content: Input filtering reduces conducted EMI: Use pi-filter or LC filter topology; Select capacitors with low ESR and high ripple current rating; Add common-mode choke for common-mode noise; Place filter close to module input pins. Typical values: 10-100uF electrolytic + 0.1-1uF ceramic + common-mode choke.

sections.heading: Output Filtering and Layout
sections.content: Output filtering reduces ripple and noise: Use low-ESR ceramic capacitors close to load; Add small inductor or ferrite bead for high-frequency noise; Keep output traces short and wide; Use ground planes for return currents. Proper layout is critical for minimizing EMI.

faqs.question: What are the main sources of EMI in DC-DC converters?
faqs.answer: Main EMI sources in DC-DC converters: 1) Switching transients: Fast switching edges (dv/dt, di/dt) generate high-frequency harmonics; 2) Diode reverse recovery: Snap-off of rectifier diodes creates ringing and noise; 3) Magnetic coupling: Transformer leakage inductance and parasitic capacitance cause coupling; 4) Common-mode noise: Parasitic capacitance between switching nodes and ground creates common-mode current; 5) Input current ripple: Pulsed input current creates conducted EMI. Understanding these sources helps target mitigation efforts effectively.
faqs.decisionGuide: Identify EMI sources through measurement, then apply targeted filtering and layout improvements.

faqs.question: How to design effective input filter for EMI reduction?
faqs.answer: Effective input filter design: 1) Topology: Use pi-filter (C-L-C) or LC filter for best attenuation; 2) Capacitors: Use 10-100uF electrolytic for low frequencies + 0.1-1uF ceramic for high frequencies; 3) Inductor: Select common-mode choke (1-10mH) for common-mode noise + differential inductor (1-10uH) for differential noise; 4) Placement: Keep filter close to module input, minimize loop area; 5) Grounding: Use single-point ground connection, avoid ground loops. Filter attenuation should be >40dB at switching frequency harmonics. Test filter effectiveness with spectrum analyzer.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use pi-filter with electrolytic and ceramic capacitors, common-mode choke, placed close to module input.

faqs.question: What is the difference between conducted and radiated EMI?
faqs.answer: Conducted EMI (150kHz-30MHz) travels through power lines and signal cables, affecting other devices connected to the same power network. It is measured using line impedance stabilization networks (LISN) and spectrum analyzers. Radiated EMI (30MHz-1GHz) emits through the air as electromagnetic waves, affecting nearby electronic devices. It is measured using antennas and anechoic chambers or GTEM cells. Conducted EMI is typically controlled with input filters, while radiated EMI requires shielding and proper PCB layout. Both types must meet regulatory limits (such as CISPR 22/32, EN 55022) for product certification.
faqs.decisionGuide: Conducted EMI controlled with filters, radiated EMI controlled with shielding and layout. Both must meet regulatory limits.

faqs.question: How does PCB layout affect EMI performance?
faqs.answer: PCB layout significantly affects EMI performance: 1) Minimize loop areas in high-frequency current paths to reduce magnetic field radiation; 2) Use ground planes to provide low-impedance return paths and reduce ground noise; 3) Keep switching nodes away from sensitive circuits and input/output connectors; 4) Place input filter components close to the module input to minimize noise coupling; 5) Use proper trace widths and avoid sharp corners to reduce impedance discontinuities; 6) Separate analog and digital grounds, connecting at a single point. Good layout can reduce EMI by 20-40dB compared to poor layout. Always follow the manufacturer's layout recommendations for best EMI performance.
faqs.decisionGuide: Minimize loop areas, use ground planes, place filters close to module, separate analog and digital grounds for best EMI performance.

faqs.question: When should I use shielding for EMI reduction?
faqs.answer: Use shielding when: 1) Input filtering and layout improvements are insufficient to meet EMI requirements; 2) The application has very strict EMI limits (such as medical or military equipment); 3) The module is installed in a sensitive environment with other electronic equipment; 4) Radiated EMI is the dominant issue rather than conducted EMI. Shielding can be implemented as: 1) Module-level shielding with metal cans over the power module; 2) Enclosure-level shielding with metal cases; 3) Cable shielding with shielded cables and proper grounding. Shielding effectiveness depends on material conductivity, thickness, and seam/joint quality. Proper shielding can provide 40-80dB of additional EMI reduction.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use shielding when filtering and layout are insufficient, for strict EMI requirements, or in sensitive environments.

author.name: Li Ming
author.title: EMC Engineer
author.bio: 10 years experience in EMC design, testing, and certification for power electronics.

articles.summary: Best practices for EMI reduction and EMC compliance in power module applications.

faeInsights.content: EMI issues are among the most challenging problems in power module applications. In my 10 years of EMC work, I've learned that prevention is much easier than cure. Proper filtering and layout from the start saves weeks of troubleshooting later. The most common mistake is inadequate input filtering - many designers use just one capacitor when a proper pi-filter is needed. Another issue is poor grounding - ground loops and noisy ground references cause common-mode problems. I always recommend pre-compliance testing early in design to catch issues before they become expensive problems. This guide summarizes the techniques that have proven most effective in my experience.
faeInsights.insightLogic: EMI mitigation requires understanding sources, applying proper filtering, and following good layout practices from the start.
keyTakeaways: Use proper pi-filter for input filtering
Place filter components close to module
bestPractices: Design filtering based on switching frequency
Use combination of electrolytic and ceramic capacitors
Minimize loop areas in high-frequency paths
Implement proper shielding for sensitive applications
troubleshootingTips: Use spectrum analyzer to identify noise frequencies
Check filter component values and placement

customerCases.challenge: Failed EMC compliance testing for medical certification
customerCases.solution: Redesigned input filter with proper pi-filter topology, improved PCB layout grounding
customerCases.feedback: Passed EMC testing with 6dB margin, obtained medical certification on schedule

articles.title: Thermal Design and Heat Dissipation Guide
articles.description: Thermal management techniques for power modules including heat sinking, derating, and thermal analysis
articles.shortDescription: Thermal management and heat dissipation for reliable power module operation
content: This technical reference document provides detailed information about aipu product specifications, characteristics, and performance parameters. Use this information to support your design and analysis activities.

Electrical characteristics are specified over the operating temperature range unless otherwise noted. Parameters are guaranteed by design, testing, or statistical analysis. Typical values represent the most likely parametric norm at 25°C.

Thermal characteristics require careful attention during system design. The junction-to-ambient thermal resistance depends on the mounting configuration, PCB copper area, and airflow conditions. Use thermal simulation tools to predict operating temperatures under actual conditions.

Reliability data is based on accelerated life testing and field failure analysis. Mean time between failures (MTBF) calculations follow industry-standard methodologies. Contact BeiLuo for detailed reliability reports and qualification data.

sections.heading: Understanding Power Module Thermal Characteristics
sections.content: Power modules generate heat due to conversion losses. Key thermal parameters: Efficiency determines power loss (P_loss = P_out × (1-η)/η); Thermal resistance (Rth) defines temperature rise per watt; Maximum junction temperature limits reliable operation. Understanding these parameters is essential for thermal design.

sections.heading: Natural Convection Cooling
sections.content: Natural convection is the simplest cooling method: Relies on air density differences from temperature gradients; Requires adequate clearance around module (typically 10mm minimum); Orientation affects performance (vertical better than horizontal); Ambient temperature directly impacts maximum power. Suitable for low-power applications (<5W) and moderate ambient temperatures.

sections.heading: Heat Sinking and Forced Air Cooling
sections.content: For higher power or high-temperature environments: Use aluminum heatsinks with thermal interface material; Calculate required heatsink thermal resistance: Rth_hs = (Tj_max - Ta)/P_loss - Rth_jc - Rth_cs; Forced air cooling significantly improves heat transfer; Consider fan reliability and noise in system design.

faqs.question: How to calculate power module temperature rise?
faqs.answer: Calculate temperature rise: 1) Determine power loss: P_loss = P_out × (1-η)/η, where η is efficiency; 2) Find thermal resistance: Rth_ja (junction to ambient) from datasheet; 3) Calculate temperature rise: ΔT = P_loss × Rth_ja; 4) Find junction temperature: Tj = Ta + ΔT; 5) Verify Tj < Tj_max (typically 125°C or 150°C). Example: 2W module at 84% efficiency, P_loss = 0.38W, Rth_ja = 60°C/W, ΔT = 22.8°C, Tj = 25°C + 22.8°C = 47.8°C (well below limit). For high ambient temperatures, apply derating.
faqs.decisionGuide: Calculate power loss from efficiency, multiply by thermal resistance to get temperature rise, verify junction temperature is within limits.

faqs.question: When is heat sinking required for power modules?
faqs.answer: Heat sinking is required when: 1) Power level is high (>5W for natural convection); 2) Ambient temperature is elevated (>50°C); 3) Enclosed space with limited air flow; 4) Continuous operation at full load; 5) Reliability is critical (lower temperature = longer life). Calculate required thermal resistance: Rth_required = (Tj_max - Ta)/P_loss - Rth_jc - Rth_cs. If Rth_required is less than natural convection can provide, add heatsink. Example: For Tj_max=125°C, Ta=70°C, P_loss=1W, Rth_jc=10°C/W, Rth_cs=1°C/W: Rth_required = (125-70)/1 - 10 - 1 = 44°C/W. Natural convection Rth is about 60°C/W, so heatsink with Rth < 44°C/W is needed.
faqs.decisionGuide: Calculate required thermal resistance. If less than natural convection provides, add heatsink with adequate thermal performance.

faqs.question: What thermal interface material should I use for heat sinks?
faqs.answer: Thermal interface materials (TIM) reduce thermal resistance between module and heatsink: 1) Thermal grease: Best performance (0.1-0.5°C·cm²/W), requires careful application; 2) Thermal pads: Easy to use, consistent thickness (0.5-3°C·cm²/W), good for production; 3) Phase change materials: Solid at room temperature, flow at operating temperature, excellent contact; 4) Thermally conductive adhesive: Permanent attachment, moderate performance. Selection guidelines: For high-power applications (>10W), use thermal grease for best performance. For production environments, use thermal pads for consistency and ease of assembly. Apply thin, even layer - excess material increases thermal resistance. Typical thickness should be 0.1-0.2mm after compression.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use thermal grease for high-power applications requiring best performance. Use thermal pads for production consistency and ease of assembly.

faqs.question: How to implement temperature monitoring for power modules?
faqs.answer: Temperature monitoring methods for power modules: 1) Internal temperature sensing: Some modules have built-in thermal shutdown, but no external monitoring; 2) External thermistor: Place NTC thermistor near module case, monitor with ADC; 3) Thermocouple: For accurate temperature measurement during testing; 4) IR thermal sensor: Non-contact monitoring for critical systems; 5) Module case temperature: Measure case temperature and estimate junction temperature using Tj = Tcase + P_loss × Rth_jc. Implementation guidelines: Place sensor close to module (within 5mm), ensure good thermal contact, use appropriate alarm thresholds (typically 10-20°C below maximum), implement graceful shutdown or load reduction when overheating. For critical applications, use redundant temperature monitoring.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use external thermistor for continuous monitoring. Use thermocouple or IR sensor for testing and validation.

faqs.question: How to select the right heat sink for my power module?
faqs.answer: Heat sink selection process: 1) Calculate required thermal resistance: Rth_required = (Tj_max - Ta)/P_loss - Rth_jc - Rth_cs; 2) Determine available space: Length, width, and height constraints; 3) Consider mounting options: Through-hole, surface mount, or adhesive attachment; 4) Evaluate airflow: Natural convection vs forced air cooling; 5) Select material: Aluminum (cost-effective) or copper (better performance). Heat sink sizing guidelines: For natural convection, allow 30-50 cm² surface area per watt. For forced air (1-2 m/s), allow 10-20 cm² per watt. Fin density should match airflow - dense fins for forced air, sparse fins for natural convection. Always verify actual temperatures in final application.
faqs.decisionGuide: Calculate required thermal resistance, then select heatsink with adequate surface area and appropriate fin density for your cooling method.

author.name: Wang Jun
author.title: Thermal Engineer
author.bio: 12 years experience in thermal design and analysis for electronic systems.

articles.summary: Thermal management techniques for reliable power module operation in various environments.

faeInsights.content: Thermal management is often overlooked in power module applications, leading to reliability issues. In my experience, most thermal failures occur not from inadequate component rating, but from poor thermal design. The most common mistake is installing high-power modules in enclosed spaces without considering heat dissipation. I always tell customers: every 10°C reduction in operating temperature doubles the lifetime. For industrial applications, plan for worst-case ambient temperatures. Natural convection works for low power, but don't hesitate to add heatsinks or fans for high power or harsh environments. This guide provides the calculation methods and practical guidelines I use in my thermal designs.
faeInsights.insightLogic: Proper thermal design requires calculating power losses, understanding thermal resistances, and selecting appropriate cooling methods.
commonPitfalls: Ignoring thermal constraints in enclosure design
bestPractices: Perform thermal calculations during design phase
Use thermal imaging to verify actual temperatures
Implement temperature monitoring for critical systems
Apply derating for high ambient temperatures
troubleshootingTips: Use thermal camera to identify hot spots
Verify air flow paths in enclosure

customerCases.challenge: Power modules overheating in outdoor cabinet during summer (ambient >60°C)
customerCases.solution: Redesigned with heatsinks and forced air cooling, implemented temperature monitoring
customerCases.feedback: Module temperatures reduced by 25°C, reliable operation maintained even at 70°C ambient
